ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Egg Makhani Masala; A0CKP (Egg dishes) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared egg dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Egg Makhani Masala in Singapore is approximately 200g, comprising two to three boiled-then-fried eggs in a generous portion of makhani sauce, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Makhani Masala — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, carbohydrate 2.36g per 100g) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Egg Makhani Masala (cr_id G00010984, confidence 0.95). Fat value of 0.0g is as reported by HPB; this is flagged as anomalous for a butter-cream dish and documented in notes. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, fibre, sodium, sugar) estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 using the closest analogous egg curry entry, and from USDA FoodData Central entry for egg curry (FDC ID 2346404) as a cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Egg Makhani Masala. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous egg-based curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 2.36g carbohydrate per 100g from tomato and onion; the low carbohydrate content and high protein from eggs strongly suppress postprandial glucose response. GL per 200g serving = (35 × 4.72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.65, rounded to 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320mg per 100g; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150g and request reduced-salt preparation, as sodium at 320mg/100g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets (1500–2000mg/day) when consumed in a full 200g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.36g/100g) and high protein (10.7g/100g) with low GI (~35) support stable postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg Makhani Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes dues to its very low carbohydrate content, high protein, and low glycemic index (~35), which together minimise postprandial glucose excursions; however, the butter and cream in makhani sauce should be requested in reduced quantities to limit saturated fat intake and avoid worsening insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol from eggs (~180mg/100g estimated) and saturated fat from butter and cream in makhani sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Egg Makhani Masala in moderation (≤150g per occasion), request reduced butter and cream in preparation, and monitor overall dietary cholesterol intake, as eggs contribute approximately 180mg cholesterol per 100g of dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) offset nightshade content (tomato, chili) in most patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric and ginger in the masala provide curcumin and gingerol with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades (tomato, chili) should monitor personal tolerance and reduce chili powder if joint flares are observed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Makhani Masala is an Indian egg dish popular in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker scene. It consists of boiled eggs that are lightly fried to develop a golden exterior, then simmered in a rich makhani (butter-tomato) sauce made from pureed tomatoes, onions, butter, cream, and a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The dish has a creamy, mildly spiced, tangy-sweet flavour profile with a silky sauce texture. Per 100g, it provides 55 kcal, 10.7g protein, 2.36g carbohydrate, and 0.0g fat as reported by HPB Singapore (the fat value is anomalously low for a butter-cream preparation and may reflect a lean restaurant variant or a data limitation). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, while the low carbohydrate load is favourable for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium at approximately 320mg/100g warrants portion control for hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free. It is typically served with naan, roti, or steamed rice and is common at lunch and dinner in Singapore's Indian cuisine establishments.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per source metadata). However, the fat value of 0.0g per 100g is anomalously low for a dish containing butter and cream, suggesting either a lean preparation variant or a data limitation in the HPB entry. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, fibre, sodium, sugar, cholesterol, potassium) are estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 and USDA FoodData Central analogous egg curry entries. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is rated medium due to the fat anomaly and estimated secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. FAT ZERO JUSTIFICATION (g4): fat_g is reported as 0.0g per 100g by the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010984, confidence 0.95). This value is anomalously low for a makhani dish containing butter and cream. It is preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) and flagged as a potential data limitation or lean-preparation variant. The HPB value is treated as 'confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database' for the purposes of this record, but downstream users should be aware that typical Egg Makhani Masala preparations contain 5–12g fat per 100g. Saturated fat is estimated at 0.3g/100g as a conservative minimum consistent with the HPB fat value. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific HPB-recorded Egg Makhani Masala preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database, ICMR-NIN 2017, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI NOTE: Glycemic index is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on egg-based curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Egg Makhani Masala was found in approved sources. 4. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: This dish is of Indian origin but is served widely in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate category-level matches; no exact ontology term exists for Egg Makhani Masala in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16.
